summary: Engineers frustrated about slow career progression often overlook that promotion opportunities are situational — not every company has the right openings, oxygen, or need at every level at every time. Title inflation from making exceptions undermines leveling systems, and engineers should strategically evaluate whether their current environment actually offers the growth opportunities they seek rather than passively waiting to be promoted.
key_points: Promotion opportunities are finite and situational — companies can only support so many senior+ engineers with meaningful high-level work
Title inflation from making exceptions destroys the integrity of leveling systems
Generalists level up faster than specialists
Engineers stuck at the senior bubble often want someone to tell them what to do, which is the opposite of what senior engineers do
It's much easier to level up at fast-growing companies where there's more work than workers
money_quotes: Not every opportunity exists at every company at every time.
Literally no company can possibly make use of a top-heavy eng org stuffed with senior+ engineers, if all of them are expected to demonstrate company-wide impact or global impact every review period.

Many engineers get stuck on the bubble getting to senior because they are impatient and want a map. They just want someone to tell them what to do. Which is the very opposite of what a senior engineer does.
The only leverage you have when people try to get you to make exceptions is if you can honestly say, 'no exceptions.' Give in just once, and your moral authority evaporates.
There is sooo much title inflation in this industry already. People are given the title 'senior engineer' in just 3-5 years, need I say more??
key_insight: Career progression depends less on individual merit and more on whether your current environment has the structural oxygen — the right ratio of work, people, and need — to support your advancement.
